message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>BACKGROUND</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>In Marseille, France, the COVID-19 incidence evolved unusually with several successive epidemic episodes. The second outbreak started in July, was associated with North Africa, and involved travelers and an outbreak on passenger ships. This suggested the involvement of a new viral variant.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>METHODS</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We sequenced the genomes from 916 SARS-CoV-2 strains from COVID-19 patients in our institute. The patients’ demographic and clinical features were compared according to the infecting viral variant.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>RESULTS</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    From June 26
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    to August 14
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    , we identified a new viral variant (Marseille-1). Based on genome sequences (n=89) or specific qPCR (n=53), 142 patients infected with this variant were detected. It is characterized by a combination of 10 mutations located in the
                    <jats:italic>nsp2, nsp3, nsp12, S, ORF3a, ORF8</jats:italic>
                    and
                    <jats:italic>N/ORF14</jats:italic>
                    genes. We identified Senegal and Gambia, where the virus had been transferred from China and Europe in February-April as the sources of the Marseille-1 variant, which then most likely reached Marseille through Maghreb when French borders reopened. In France, this variant apparently remained almost limited to Marseille. In addition, it was significantly associated with a milder disease compared to clade 20A ancestor strains.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>CONCLUSION</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Our results demonstrate that SARS-CoV-2 can genetically diversify rapidly, its variants can diffuse internationally and cause successive outbreaks.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Introduction into the Marseille geographical area of a mild SARS-CoV-2 variant originating from sub-Saharan Africa
